full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> One of the most interesting and sometimes difficult lessons in life is to realize that<|6.34|> <|6.34|> two opposites are often true at the <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> talk about the death penalty and his hopes for abolishing it<|5.00|> <|5.60|> and his hopes for criminal justice reform.<|8.86|> <|8.86|> And he said a line I will never forget,<|12.64|> <|12.64|> that each of us is more than the worst thing<|16.72|> <|16.72|> we have ever done.<|18.48|> <|18.48|> And I think he followed that up with<|20.64|> <|20.64|> that each of us is not quite as good<|23.54|> <|23.54|> as the greatest thing we've ever done.<|25.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.40|> This paradox of opposites, especially regarding human nature, is so true of Palm Sunday.<|8.32|> <|8.32|> In this first gospel reading, which is often called the triumphal entry of Jesus into Jerusalem,<|14.08|> <|14.08|> Jesus is being lauded by crowds of people, he's entering into the capital city,<|21.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There is the colt, there is the donkey, there is pageantry, there is palms and all of that.<|8.16|> <|8.16|> And they are, the crowds are yelling, Hosanna to their king.<|13.90|> <|14.26|> And they mean it.<|15.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We do something similar in the Episcopal Church all the time.<|5.50|> <|5.50|> We bring our heartfelt ritual and prayers to Jesus the Christ, and all of it really matters to us.<|16.00|> <|16.00|> These Holy Week prayers, for example, really are not just mere ritual but speak to the depths of our heart.<|23.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and of our mind as we celebrate Jesus is upon the cross. Jesus is about to die.<|6.00|> <|6.80|> Jesus even questions if God, not just the crowds, have forsaken him.<|12.38|> <|12.44|> And that most haunting of all questions that Jesus asks,<|15.46|> <|16.12|> my God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?<|19.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.92|> This second reading relates to something that is so true about us as the church,<|9.12|> <|9.12|> something so true about us collectively and individually.<|12.90|> <|13.36|> Even though we celebrate and laud and worship Jesus the Christ,<|18.16|> <|18.74|> we don't quite go as far as the Christ calls us to go.<|24.50|> <|25.12|> We don't always seek and serve Christ individually.<|29.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and all persons as the baptismal covenant calls us to do.<|4.28|> <|4.28|> We have not arrived yet.<|7.00|> <|8.94|> So on Palm Sunday, these two things are true.<|13.94|> <|16.30|> We celebrate and pray to Jesus the Christ,<|18.92|> <|18.92|> giving great thanks, heartfelt thanks,<|21.66|> <|21.66|> through ritual word and one day again, sacrament.<|25.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> for Christ who is our King, who is our ruler, who is the center of the entire<|9.72|> <|9.72|> cosmos and yet we never follow Christ completely in the ways that we<|16.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> should. And yet the great beauty and tenderness of this day is that Christ knows that both of those<|7.22|> <|7.22|> things are true of us and of the church. And even still, Christ invites our worship<|14.48|> <|14.48|> and our self-awareness coupled with honesty.<|19.94|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.42|><|endoftext|>
